text.title: Security Testing Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common security testing failures: assert != 500, testing only happy paths, hardcoded test credentials.

antipattern.reason: This passes when the server returns 200 with the victim's data. The test confirms "no crash," not "correctly denied." Always assert the exact expected denial code (401/403/400) and that the protected resource is absent from the response body.

antipattern.description: Only testing happy paths — no tests for attack scenarios (injection, path traversal, privilege escalation).
antipattern.reason: Happy-path test coverage gives false confidence. Security vulnerabilities live in edge cases and boundary conditions that are never exercised by tests that only simulate legitimate users.

antipattern.description: Hardcoded real or realistic-looking credentials in test files (API keys, passwords, AWS keys).
antipattern.reason: The moment the repository goes public (or is accessed by a contractor), hardcoded credentials become real secrets. Use placeholder values that cannot be mistaken for real credentials (e.g., `"test-password-do-not-use"`).

antipattern.description: Security testing as a one-time pre-launch activity rather than an automated CI gate.
antipattern.reason: Every new feature reintroduces vulnerability classes. Without continuous automated scanning, security degrades incrementally and invisibly between manual audits.

antipattern.description: Skipping low-severity findings because they "don't matter."
antipattern.reason: Low-severity issues frequently combine with each other to produce high-severity exploits (chained vulnerabilities). Every finding should be triaged, not ignored by default.
